subject: mathematics

sections.title: Mind the Mistake, Mend the Mistake

prose_summary:
Section 15.1 applies balance reasoning to find unknowns and then introduces the language of equations. It opens with a series of weighing-scale pictures whose two plates balance, asking students to find unknown weights, and gives the key idea through a hint: removing equal weights from both plates keeps the scale balanced. It then turns to a matchstick sequence whose position n uses 2n + 1 sticks, so finding which arrangement uses 99 sticks means solving 2n + 1 = 99. This motivates the definition of an equation as a statement of equality between two algebraic expressions written with an equal sign between them, with the Left Hand Side (LHS) and Right Hand Side (RHS), and solving meaning finding the letter-number value for which the LHS equals the RHS. Several examples of equations (3x + 4 = 7, 20 = y − 3, a/3 = 50, 2z + 4 = 5z − 14) are given. Students revisit the balance pictures — denoting an unknown weight by a letter — to frame equations such as 2e = 6 and 4 + 2y = 16, solve them, check the solutions against the weights they found earlier, and are asked to frame and solve five equations of their own.

Section 15.2 applies systematic methods to solve equations and reasons about why they work. It first shows the trial-and-error method (substituting values until LHS = RHS), works it through for 2n + 1 = 99 to reach n = 49, and notes its inefficiency, then establishes that performing the same operation on both sides of an equation preserves equality — the algebraic version of the balance property. Through arithmetic Examples 1–4 it uses inverse operations (subtracting a term, dividing by a factor, adding an additive inverse, dividing by a fractional factor) to isolate an expression, then solves 5x − 4 = 7, 11y + (−5) = 61, and 6y + 7 = 4y + 21, checking solutions by substitution and bringing unknown terms to one side. It distils the shortcut rules of transposition — a term added or subtracted on one side, when removed, appears as its additive inverse on the other side (so 2y + 7 = 21 becomes 2y = 21 − 7); a factor removed from a product means the other side is divided by it (2y = 14 becomes y = 14 ÷ 2); and a divisor removed means the other side is multiplied by it (u/15 = 6 becomes u = 6 × 15). It then forms and solves equations for real problems: Ranjana's square-tile pattern (3k + 1 = 100), Madhubanti's party budget (solved several ways by different children), Jahnavi and Sunita's equal savings after 7 months, 28(x + 4) + 300 = 1000 solved by several routes, Riyaz's think-of-a-number trick, and Ramesh and Suresh's marbles (reducing two unknowns to one). A 'Generating Equations' part reverses the process, asking students to write equations with a given solution and to build chains of equivalent equations by performing the same operation on both sides, and Example 13 asks for a real-life situation modelling 100x + 75 = 250. Its Figure it Out sets solve varied equations, frame equations with a given solution or no solution, and pose digit, brick, and fraction puzzles.

Section 15.3 reasons critically about worked solutions. It presents nine equations, each already solved with the steps shown, and asks students to decide whether each step is correct, describe any mistake (such as moving a term without changing its sign or dropping a coefficient), correct it, and solve the equation properly — turning the balance and transposition rules into a tool for critical error analysis rather than mere procedure-following.

Section 15.4 recalls the history of algebra and applies an ancient formula. It explains that forming and solving equations was called bījagaṇita (bīja meaning seed) in ancient India, traces Brahmagupta's Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta (628 CE) as one of the earliest works of algebra, the transmission of Indian ideas to Al-Khwarizmi (c. 825 CE, whose al-jabr gives us the word algebra), and the ancient Indian notation using symbols such as yā, kā, and rū for unknowns and knowns. It notes David Mumford's remark that 'Brahmagupta is the key person in the creation of algebra as we know it'. Bhāskarāchārya's horse-and-debt problem (Example 16) is modelled and solved, and Brahmagupta's general formula x = (D − B)/(A − C) for equations of the form Ax + B = Cx + D — a method first proposed by Aryabhata and outlined by Brahmagupta — is stated and applied, and the section closes by noting that algebra lets us generalise patterns and justify claims. Its extensive Figure it Out set covers fill-in-the-blank equations, wage, taxi-fare, restaurant, angle, and grill problems, the Bakhśhāli Manuscript (300 CE) distribution problem, stick-pattern sequences, a solve-your-path maze, and a heads-and-feet puzzle about children and donkeys.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: Given 14593 − 1459 + 145 − 14 + 88 = 13353, find 14593 − 1459 + 145 − 14 by subtracting 88 from both sides (addition and subtraction as inverse operations): the value is 13265.
Given 23 × 41 × 11 × 8 × 7 = 5,80,888, find 23 × 41 × 11 × 8 by dividing 5,80,888 by 7 (multiplication and division as inverse operations).
Given 12345 − 5432 + 135 − 24 − (−67) = 7091, find 12345 − 5432 + 135 − 24 by adding (−67) to both sides to remove the −(−67) term: the value is 7024.
Given (35/113 × 24 × 14 × 8/9) = 94080/1017, find (35/113 × 24 × 14) by dividing both sides by 8/9 (multiplying by 9/8): the value is 11760/113.
Solve 11y + (−5) = 61 by subtracting (−5) from both sides to get 11y = 66, then dividing by 11: y = 6.
Solve 6y + 7 = 4y + 21 by subtracting 4y from both sides to get 2y + 7 = 21, then 2y = 14 and y = 7.
Ranjana's square-tile sequence uses 3k + 1 tiles at Step k (shown by two counting methods); to make 100 tiles, solve 3k + 1 = 100 for k.
Madhubanti's party: snacks cost ₹25 per plate plus ₹50 delivery, budget ₹500, 5 family members. Solved several ways (25p + 50 = 500; 25(f + 5) = 450): 18 plates, so 13 friends can be invited.
Jahnavi (₹4000 + ₹650/month) and Sunita (₹5050 + ₹500/month) have equal savings when 4000 + 650m = 5050 + 500m, giving 150m = 1050 and m = 7 months.
Solve 28(x + 4) + 300 = 1000 by several routes (subtract 300 then divide by 28; divide throughout by 4; or expand): x = 21.
Riyaz's number trick (think of x, subtract 3, multiply by 4, add 8) gives 4x − 4; with final answer 24, solve 4x − 4 = 24 to find the starting number x = 7.
Ramesh and Suresh have 60 marbles, Ramesh 30 more than Suresh; letting Suresh have y and Ramesh y + 30 gives y + (y + 30) = 60, i.e. 2y + 30 = 60, to find both unknowns.
Give a real-life situation modelled by 100x + 75 = 250 — for example, 100 units at cost x per unit plus a fixed ₹75 delivery charge totalling ₹250.

Bhāskarāchārya's Bījgaṇita (1150 CE) problem: one man has ₹300 and 6 horses, another has 10 horses and a ₹100 debt, and they are equally rich; solving 300 + 6x = 10x − 100 gives the price of one horse x = ₹100.

Figure it Out Q2, section 15.2 p.171
enumerated_exercises.description: Frame an equation that has no solution.

enumerated_exercises.description: Write 5 equations whose solution is x = −2.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 15.2 p.180
Figure it Out Q3, section 15.2 p.180
enumerated_exercises.description: A 3-digit number's hundreds digit is 3 less than its tens digit, its tens digit is 3 less than its units digit, and the digits sum to 15; find the number.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 15.2 p.180
enumerated_exercises.description: The weight of a brick is 1 kg more than half its weight; find the weight of the brick.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 15.2 p.180
enumerated_exercises.description: One quarter of a number increased by 9 gives the same number; find the number.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 15.2 p.180
section 15.3 p.181–182
enumerated_exercises.description: For nine given worked solutions (e.g. 4x + 6 = 10, 7 − 8z = 5, 2v − 4 = 6, 5z + 2 = 3z − 4, 15w − 4w = 26, 3x + 1 = −12, 4(4q + 2) = 50, −2(3 − 4x) = 14, 3(7y + 4) = 9 + 5y), decide whether the steps are correct, describe any mistake, correct it, and solve the equation.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 15.4 p.185
Figure it Out Q2, section 15.4 p.185
enumerated_exercises.description: Ranju earns ₹750 a day, paid in an equal number of ₹50 and ₹100 notes; find how many notes of each she has.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 15.4 p.185
enumerated_exercises.description: Each black blob hides an equal number of blue dots; if there are 25 dots in total, write an equation and find how many dots one blob covers.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 15.4 p.185–186
enumerated_exercises.description: For the given input-operation-output machines (parts a and b), find the inputs that produce the stated outputs.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 15.4 p.186
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the inputs to the given machines from their outputs.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 15.4 p.186
enumerated_exercises.description: A taxi charges ₹800 fixed per day plus ₹20 per kilometre; if the total is ₹2200, find the kilometres travelled.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q7, section 15.4 p.187
enumerated_exercises.description: The sum of two numbers is 76 and one is three times the other; find the numbers.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q8, section 15.4 p.187
enumerated_exercises.description: From the window-grill diagram (34 cm span with 3 cm and 2 cm markings), find the gap between two rods in the grill.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q9, section 15.4 p.187
enumerated_exercises.description: A fruit juice costs ₹15 less than a chocolate milkshake; if 4 juices and 7 milkshakes cost ₹600, find the cost of each.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q10, section 15.4 p.187
enumerated_exercises.description: Given 28p − 36 = 98, find the values of 14p − 19 and 28p − 38.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q11, section 15.4 p.187
enumerated_exercises.description: Identify and correct any mistakes in the given solution steps of (a) 6x + 9 = 66; (b) 14y + 24 = 36; (c) 4x − 5 = 9x + 8.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q12, section 15.4 p.187
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the measures of the angles of the two given triangles (with angles expressed as y and y + 15, and x, x − 10, x + 10).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q13, section 15.4 p.188
enumerated_exercises.description: Write 4 equations whose solution is u = 6.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q14, section 15.4 p.188
enumerated_exercises.description: Bakhśhāli Manuscript (300 CE) problem: the second person gets twice the first, the third thrice the second, the fourth four times the third, and the total distributed is 132; find the first person's amount.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q15, section 15.4 p.188
enumerated_exercises.description: The height of a giraffe is two and a half metres more than half its height; find how tall the giraffe is.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q16, section 15.4 p.188
enumerated_exercises.description: For two stick-arrangement sequences, find (a) squares at position 11; (b) sticks at position 11; (c) whether 85 sticks is achievable and at which position; (d) whether 150 sticks is achievable and at which position.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q17, section 15.4 p.188
enumerated_exercises.description: A number increased by 36 equals ten times itself; find the number.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q18, section 15.4 p.188
Figure it Out Q19, section 15.4 p.188–189
enumerated_exercises.description: Solve the equations in the maze grid to find and colour a path from Start to End, showing the work for each step.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q20, section 15.4 p.189
enumerated_exercises.description: Children and donkeys on a beach together have 28 heads and 80 feet; find how many donkeys and how many children there are.
